topic: HSRA, Bhagat Singh, INA & Subhas Chandra Bose

questions.text: Bhagat Singh threw a bomb in the Central Legislative Assembly on 8 April 1929. Who was his companion in this act, and why did they NOT try to escape?
options: Companion: Rajguru; they did not escape because they were surrounded by armed police with no exit
Companion: Sukhdev; they surrendered because the bombs failed to explode and they had no means of escape
Companion: Batukeshwar Dutt; they deliberately chose not to escape — the arrest was part of the statement, so that their trial could be a platform to spread revolutionary ideas
Companion: Chandrashekhar Azad; they surrendered after negotiations with the Speaker of the Assembly
questions.explanation: Bhagat Singh's companion in the Assembly bomb act was Batukeshwar Dutt — NOT Rajguru, NOT Sukhdev (though both were HSRA comrades). The choice not to escape was entirely deliberate. The bombs were weak — designed to make noise, not kill (the slogan on the leaflets they threw: 'It takes a loud noise to make the deaf hear'). Bhagat Singh and Dutt stood, shouted 'Inquilab Zindabad,' and waited to be arrested. They understood that their trial would give them a national platform to spread revolutionary ideas — an opportunity that running away would deny. The trial did exactly what they expected: it made Bhagat Singh a national celebrity while he was in prison and turned his execution into one of the most impactful events of the independence movement.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Assembly bomb (8 April 1929), companion = Batukeshwar Dutt (not Rajguru/Sukhdev), and the deliberate non-escape are the three most tested facts from this act

questions.text: Bhagat Singh, Sukhdev, and Rajguru were hanged in connection with the Lahore Conspiracy Case. What was the specific act for which they were tried?
options: The Central Legislative Assembly bomb throwing on 8 April 1929
The Kakori train robbery of 9 August 1925
The killing of John P. Saunders (Deputy Superintendent of Police) in Lahore on 17 December 1928
The attempted assassination of Viceroy Lord Irwin's train in December 1929
questions.explanation: The Lahore Conspiracy Case was specifically about the killing of John P. Saunders (Deputy Superintendent of Police) in Lahore on 17 December 1928. Bhagat Singh and Rajguru had intended to kill Police Superintendent James Scott, who had ordered the lathi charge on Lala Lajpat Rai. They mistook Saunders for Scott and shot him instead. Chandrashekhar Azad provided armed cover. All three escaped from Lahore initially. The Assembly bomb case (8 April 1929) was a separate case — for that, Bhagat Singh and Batukeshwar Dutt received life sentences; the Saunders murder (Lahore Conspiracy Case) is what brought the death sentence. Bhagat Singh, Sukhdev, and Rajguru were all hanged at Lahore Central Jail on 23 March 1931.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Lahore Conspiracy Case (killing Saunders, not the Assembly bomb) as the actual charge for which they were hanged is a critical distinction; students often confuse the Assembly bomb with the hanging

questions.text: Subhas Chandra Bose won the 1939 INC presidential election against the Gandhi-backed candidate. Who was that candidate, and what was the session called?
options: Candidate: Rajendra Prasad; Session: Haripura
Candidate: Pattabhi Sitaramayya; Session: Tripuri
Candidate: Vallabhbhai Patel; Session: Lucknow
Candidate: C. Rajagopalachari; Session: Faizpur
questions.explanation: The 1939 INC presidential election is one of the most dramatic in Congress history. At the Tripuri Session (1939), Subhas Chandra Bose was re-elected as president against Pattabhi Sitaramayya — Gandhi's preferred candidate. The vote: Bose 1,580 vs. Sitaramayya 1,377. Gandhi famously said: 'Sitaramayya's defeat is my own defeat.' Despite winning, Bose faced sustained opposition from Gandhi's allies in the Congress Working Committee, all of whom refused to work with him. Unable to form a working committee, he resigned from the presidency in April 1939. He then founded the Forward Bloc in May 1939. At the Haripura Session (1938), Bose had been elected unopposed — the Tripuri drama was his SECOND election.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Tripuri Session 1939 (Bose vs. Pattabhi Sitaramayya, Gandhi's statement about his own defeat) is among the most tested Congress leadership facts

questions.text: The INA Trials (1945-46) were held at which location, and who were the three officers first tried?
options: Location: Lahore Fort; Officers: Bhulabhai Desai, Tej Bahadur Sapru, Jawaharlal Nehru
Location: Red Fort, Delhi; Officers: Shah Nawaz Khan (Muslim), P.K. Sehgal (Hindu), G.S. Dhillon (Sikh)
Location: Singapore; Officers: Mohan Singh, Rash Behari Bose, Lakshmi Sahgal
Location: Andaman Islands; Officers: three unnamed INA captains tried in camera
questions.explanation: The INA Trials were held at Red Fort, Delhi — a deliberately symbolic choice by both sides (the British saw it as fitting; nationalists saw it as turning the Mughal Empire's seat of power into a court that would embarrass the British). The three officers selected as the first defendants were Shah Nawaz Khan (Muslim), P.K. Sehgal (Hindu), and G.S. Dhillon (Sikh) — explicitly selected to represent India's three major communities, making any conviction look like an attack on all of India, not just the INA. The defence was led by Bhulabhai Desai; Jawaharlal Nehru also appeared (wearing his barrister's gown for the first time in 25 years). The trials backfired: instead of discrediting INA soldiers as traitors, they made them heroes and the British eventually released the accused. The names in option A are the defence lawyers, not the defendants.
questions.source: SSC CGL — INA Trials (Red Fort, Shah Nawaz Khan/Sehgal/Dhillon, Bhulabhai Desai as defence counsel) is a frequently tested multi-part identification; lawyers vs. defendants is the standard confusion trap

questions.text: The Kakori Train Robbery (1925) led to the hanging of four revolutionaries. Which of the following correctly pairs the revolutionary with a notable detail about him?
options: Ram Prasad Bismil — he was Muslim; famous for the verse 'Sarfaroshi ki tamanna ab hamare dil mein hai'
Ashfaqullah Khan — he was Muslim; his Hindu comrade Ram Prasad Bismil was hanged alongside him; their friendship symbolised Hindu-Muslim revolutionary unity
Rajendra Lahiri — he was the youngest of the four; he was hanged two days before the other three on 17 December 1927 at Gondа jail
Roshan Singh — he organised the Kakori robbery as a solo operation without HRA knowledge
questions.explanation: Option B is correct and captures the most tested detail. Ashfaqullah Khan was Muslim; Ram Prasad Bismil was Hindu. Their close friendship and their execution together (December 1927 at Gorakhpur and Faizabad jails) became an iconic symbol of Hindu-Muslim unity in revolutionary nationalism — at a time when Hindu-Muslim tensions were rising across India. Option A misidentifies: Ram Prasad Bismil was Hindu (NOT Muslim), and 'Sarfaroshi ki tamanna' is attributed to Bismil (some sources credit the Urdu poet Bismil Azimabadi). Option C has a true element — Rajendra Lahiri was hanged two days early at Gonda jail on 17 December 1927; the others were hanged on 19 December — but the 'youngest' claim needs verification. This makes B the cleanest, most testable correct option.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because Kakori (1925) is tested for the four martyrs with an emphasis on the Hindu-Muslim angle (Bismil + Ashfaqullah); the 'Sarfaroshi' attribution and Lahiri's early hanging are harder details in higher-difficulty papers

questions.text: The first Indian National Army (1942) was organised by Captain Mohan Singh. How was this different from the INA that Subhas Chandra Bose reorganised in 1943?
options: The first INA had only British Indian Army POWs; Bose's INA additionally included civilian Indian volunteers from Southeast Asia and a dedicated women's regiment
The first INA and Bose's INA were identical — Mohan Singh served under Bose from 1943 onwards
The first INA was based in India and fought the Quit India Movement; Bose's INA was based in Southeast Asia
The first INA was Japanese-controlled with Indian figureheads; Bose's INA had no Japanese involvement at all
questions.explanation: Captain Mohan Singh organised the first INA in 1942 from Indian POWs captured after Singapore's fall to Japan (February 1942). It was essentially an army of POWs with unclear command relationships with the Japanese — Mohan Singh eventually dissolved it in December 1942 over disputes about Japanese intentions and whether the INA was truly independent or just a Japanese tool. When Subhas Chandra Bose arrived in Singapore in July 1943, he restructured the organisation comprehensively: (1) Brought in Indian civilian volunteers from the large Indian community in Southeast Asia (Malaya, Burma, Singapore) — not just POWs; (2) Established the Rani of Jhansi Regiment — a dedicated women's regiment under Captain Lakshmi Sahgal; (3) Created the Azad Hind Government (21 October 1943) to give the INA political legitimacy; (4) Instilled a new spirit through his personal charisma and speeches. Mohan Singh was released from Japanese custody and continued to serve but in a subordinate role.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because 'who organised the INA' is a frequent question and Mohan Singh vs. Subhas Bose is the key distinction; students often give credit only to Bose for the entire INA

questions.text: Chandrashekhar Azad died on 27 February 1931 at Alfred Park, Allahabad. What is the significance of the date relative to other events of 1931?
options: He died 4 days AFTER Bhagat Singh was hanged — Azad had been trying to arrange Bhagat Singh's escape when surrounded by police
He died 24 days BEFORE Bhagat Singh was hanged on 23 March 1931; the loss of Azad — the HSRA's chief organiser — made any rescue attempt for Bhagat Singh effectively impossible
He died on the same day as the Gandhi-Irwin Pact was signed — 27 February 1931 — making it a doubly controversial day in nationalist memory
He died 3 days after the Lahore Conspiracy Case verdict was delivered
questions.explanation: Chandrashekhar Azad died on 27 February 1931 — exactly 24 days before Bhagat Singh, Sukhdev, and Rajguru were hanged on 23 March 1931. The timing is significant: there had been discussions among HSRA members about attempting to rescue Bhagat Singh from Lahore Central Jail. Azad was the military organiser who could have planned and led such an attempt. His death at Alfred Park — fighting police who surrounded him based on an informant's tip — removed the one man who might have attempted a rescue. Whether a rescue was genuinely feasible is debated by historians, but the proximity of dates means Azad's death (27 Feb) preceded the Gandhi-Irwin Pact (5 March) and the hanging (23 March) in rapid succession — the entire HSRA leadership structure collapsed within weeks.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because the 27 February date is tested in isolation, but the significance relative to the 23 March hanging (24 days earlier, removing any rescue possibility) is the analytical connection that higher-difficulty questions require

questions.text: The Rani of Jhansi Regiment was the women's regiment of the INA. Who commanded it, and what was her background?
options: Commanded by Sarojini Naidu, who had left Congress to join the INA after being inspired by Bose's 'Give me blood' speech
Commanded by Captain Lakshmi Sahgal (Dr. Lakshmi), a doctor from Chennai who was working in Singapore when the INA was formed; she later became a prominent physician and 2002 Presidential candidate
Commanded by Aruna Asaf Ali, who had been underground since the Quit India Movement and secretly joined the INA in Burma
Commanded by Vijayalakshmi Pandit, Nehru's sister, who resigned from Congress to protest Gandhi's withdrawal of CDM
questions.explanation: The Rani of Jhansi Regiment was commanded by Captain Lakshmi Sahgal (born Lakshmi Swaminathan, 1914–2012) — a doctor from Chennai (Madras) who had gone to Singapore to work. When Singapore fell to Japan in February 1942 and the INA was formed, she joined and eventually commanded the women's regiment named after the iconic 1857 queen. The regiment included nurses, support personnel, and combatants — women who had volunteered from the large Indian civilian community in Malaya and Singapore. Captain Lakshmi returned to India after the war, was arrested briefly, and went on to become a prominent physician in Kanpur. In 2002, she was the presidential candidate put forward by the Left parties and many in Congress — she lost to A.P.J. Abdul Kalam but received significant votes. She was active in social causes until her death at 98.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because 'Rani of Jhansi Regiment' appears as a one-line fact; Captain Lakshmi Sahgal's identity (doctor from Chennai, later 2002 Presidential candidate) is the specific crossover detail tested in higher-difficulty papers

questions.text: Bhagat Singh wrote the essay 'Why I Am an Atheist' while in prison awaiting execution. What was its significance beyond its content?
options: It was significant because it was the first atheist text ever written by an Indian nationalist, making Bhagat Singh the founder of rationalist thought in India

It was significant because it demonstrated that Bhagat Singh was not just a romantic revolutionary but a serious intellectual — a 23-year-old facing death who could write a sustained philosophical argument; it also complicated the British characterisation of him as a mindless terrorist
It was significant because it was smuggled to Gandhi, who read it and changed his own religious views
It was significant because it was the last piece of evidence in his trial and directly led to his death sentence
questions.explanation: 'Why I Am an Atheist' (1930-31, written in Lahore Central Jail) was significant on multiple levels: (1) Intellectual depth — Bhagat Singh engaged with the standard religious arguments for God's existence (first cause, design, consolation in suffering) and methodically refuted each; his engagement with Marx, Bakunin, and European philosophy showed a thinker, not just an activist; (2) Counter-narrative — the British and moderate nationalists portrayed revolutionary nationalists as irresponsible, emotional, and destructive; this essay made it impossible to dismiss Bhagat Singh as a mere hothead; (3) Legacy — the essay is still in print and widely read, making it arguably one of the most widely read works of rational/atheist thought in Indian political tradition; (4) Humanity — written by a man who knew he would be hanged within months, the essay's calm philosophical tone is striking. The essay, along with his letters from prison, helped cement Bhagat Singh's posthumous identity as a revolutionary-intellectual.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because 'Why I Am an Atheist' appears as a title in lists but its significance (Bhagat Singh as thinker, not just actor; the essay's post-execution impact) is tested in analytical questions about what made Bhagat Singh different from other revolutionaries

questions.text: What was the Royal Indian Navy (RIN) Mutiny of February 1946, and how did it connect to the INA's legacy?
options: RIN sailors mutinied specifically to free INA prisoners held in Bombay Harbour; the mutiny succeeded in freeing 47 prisoners before being suppressed

On 18 February 1946, sailors of HMIS Talwar (Bombay signal school) mutinied over poor conditions and racial discrimination; the mutiny spread to 78 ships and 20 shore establishments; the mutineers flew the flags of Congress, Muslim League, and the INA together — showing that the INA's spirit of composite nationalism had infected even the Crown's own military
The RIN Mutiny was a Japanese-inspired act; Japanese agents who had worked with the INA were secretly funding and directing the mutineers through INA networks
The RIN Mutiny was a protest against Gandhi's proposal to accept Dominion Status rather than Purna Swaraj in the upcoming independence negotiations
questions.explanation: The Royal Indian Navy Mutiny began on 18 February 1946 on HMIS Talwar (a signal school ship in Bombay) over grievances about food quality, racial discrimination by British officers, and treatment of Indian sailors. It rapidly spread: 78 ships and 20 shore establishments in Bombay, Karachi, Calcutta, Madras, and elsewhere. At its peak, about 20,000 sailors were involved. The mutineers' symbolic act — flying the flags of Congress, Muslim League, and the INA together on their ships — was a direct echo of the INA's message: India's military belonged to India, not the British Empire, and Hindu-Muslim-Sikh unity was possible in the cause of independence. The INA Trials (1945) had already demonstrated this fusion sentiment. The mutiny was suppressed with some deaths; Sardar Patel and Gandhi urged surrender (which divided opinion). But its impact on British decision-making was significant: reports from the Viceroy and British military commanders to London emphasised that the loyalty of Indian troops could no longer be assumed.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because RIN Mutiny appears as a one-line fact (February 1946) but its connection to INA sentiment and its political impact on British decision-making are tested in analytical questions about the final phase of independence

questions.text:
Match the revolutionary figure with their associated act, date, or organisation:
1. Ram Prasad Bismil      A. Assembly bomb, 8 April 1929
2. Bhagat Singh           B. Kakori Train Robbery, 9 August 1925
3. Chandrashekhar Azad    C. Shot himself at Alfred Park, Allahabad, 27 February 1931
4. Batukeshwar Dutt       D. Threw Assembly bomb alongside Bhagat Singh
questions.explanation: Correct matches: (1) Ram Prasad Bismil → B. Kakori Train Robbery, 9 August 1925 — he was the lead organiser; was hanged in December 1927 along with Ashfaqullah Khan and others. (2) Bhagat Singh → A. Assembly bomb, 8 April 1929 — threw the bomb in the Central Legislative Assembly, Delhi, with Batukeshwar Dutt; chose to be arrested to use the trial as a platform. (3) Chandrashekhar Azad → C. Shot himself at Alfred Park, Allahabad, 27 February 1931 — fulfilling his vow never to be captured alive by the British. (4) Batukeshwar Dutt → D. Threw Assembly bomb alongside Bhagat Singh — NOT Rajguru, NOT Sukhdev (both were hanged in 1931 for the Saunders case, not the Assembly bomb). The Bhagat Singh-Batukeshwar Dutt pairing (Assembly bomb) vs. Bhagat Singh-Rajguru pairing (Saunders killing) is the most commonly tested distinction in this topic.
questions.source: Revolutionary figure matching (Bismil/Kakori, Bhagat Singh/Assembly bomb, Azad/Alfred Park, Batukeshwar Dutt/Assembly bomb companion) is the single most frequently tested question format for this topic

questions.text: Which of the following statements about Subhas Chandra Bose and the INA is INCORRECT?
options: Bose founded the Forward Bloc in May 1939 after resigning from the Congress presidency
The Azad Hind Government (Provisional Government of Free India) was established on 21 October 1943 in Singapore
The INA included three brigades named after Gandhi, Nehru, and Azad, and a women's regiment called the Rani of Jhansi Regiment
Bose was elected INC President at the Tripuri Session (1939) for the first time, defeating Rajendra Prasad
questions.explanation: The INCORRECT statement is option D. At the Tripuri Session (1939), Bose was elected for the SECOND time (not the first), and his opponent was Pattabhi Sitaramayya (not Rajendra Prasad). Bose had first been elected at the Haripura Session (1938) — unopposed. His second, contested election at Tripuri 1939 against Gandhi's candidate Sitaramayya is the famous one. The other three options are correct: Forward Bloc in May 1939 after resigning (A); Azad Hind Government on 21 October 1943 in Singapore (B); three brigades named Gandhi, Nehru, Azad + Rani of Jhansi Regiment (C). The 'first time vs. second time' and 'Sitaramayya vs. Rajendra Prasad' are the two specific traps in option D.
questions.source: INCORRECT statement format for Subhas Bose/INA (Tripuri = second election, opponent = Sitaramayya not Rajendra Prasad) is a standard SSC trap; options A-C are consistently used as true distractors

questions.text: What was the Naujawan Bharat Sabha (1926) and who founded it?
options: A Congress youth wing founded by Jawaharlal Nehru to mobilise students during the Non-Cooperation Movement
A revolutionary youth organisation founded by Bhagat Singh in Lahore to organise working-class and peasant youth outside the Congress framework; it was linked to the HSRA
A cultural organisation founded by Rabindranath Tagore to preserve Indian youth literature and drama during the colonial period
An underground network founded by Chandrashekhar Azad to recruit members for armed revolutionary activities in the United Provinces
questions.explanation: Naujawan Bharat Sabha (Young India Society) was founded by Bhagat Singh in Lahore in 1926. Its purpose was to mobilise working-class and peasant youth in a revolutionary-socialist direction, outside the moderate Congress framework. It engaged in social work, organised workers, and discussed radical political ideas. It was distinct from but connected to the HSRA — it served as a wider recruitment and awareness-raising body while the HSRA was the armed inner core. The Naujawan Bharat Sabha is tested in questions that distinguish between Bhagat Singh's various activities: HSRA (armed revolutionary organisation), Naujawan Bharat Sabha (mass youth organisation), and specific acts (Saunders killing, Assembly bomb).
questions.source: Naujawan Bharat Sabha (1926, Bhagat Singh, Lahore, working-class youth outside Congress) is a frequently tested identification question that students confuse with the HSRA or with Congress's own youth wings
